question_text: The Mandal Commission was officially named the Second Backward Classes Commission and was constituted in 1979 under the chairmanship of which leader?

options.text: Jagjivan Ram
B.P. Mandal
V.P. Singh
Charan Singh

explanation: The Second Backward Classes Commission was headed by Bindeshwari Prasad Mandal, a Bihar politician from the Yadav community, after whom the commission came to be popularly known as the Mandal Commission. It was constituted by the Janata Party government under Prime Minister Morarji Desai in December 1978 and submitted its report in 1980. The commission recommended 27 percent reservation for Other Backward Classes in central government jobs and educational institutions in addition to the existing 22.5 percent reservation for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.
elimination_hints: Jagjivan Ram was a prominent Dalit leader and Congress politician, not the Mandal Commission chair.
V.P. Singh implemented the Mandal Commission recommendations in 1990 but did not head the commission.
Charan Singh was Prime Minister briefly in 1979-80 but the commission was chaired by B.P. Mandal.
subject: Political Science

question_text: V.P. Singh's government announced the implementation of the Mandal Commission report in August 1990, recommending reservation for OBCs in central government jobs at what percentage?

options.text: 15 percent
22 percent
27 percent
33 percent

explanation: The Mandal Commission recommended 27 percent reservation for Other Backward Classes in central government services and public sector undertakings. This recommendation, when implemented by V.P. Singh in August 1990, would bring the total reservation in central government employment to 49.5 percent when combined with the existing 22.5 percent for SC/ST communities. The 27 percent figure was calculated to keep total reservations just under 50 percent, in line with the Supreme Court's earlier observations about reservation limits. This announcement triggered massive protests across North India, particularly among upper-caste students and youth.
elimination_hints: 15 percent would be too low to represent the OBC population proportion; 27 percent was the commission's recommendation.
22 percent was the existing SC/ST combined reservation; OBC reservation was 27 percent on top of this.
33 percent would breach the 50 percent ceiling when added to SC/ST reservations; 27 percent keeps total at 49.5 percent.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The anti-Mandal agitation of 1990 became intensely emotional when upper-caste students engaged in which dramatic protest that shocked the nation and received extensive media coverage?

options.text: Mass burning of Mandal Commission report copies outside Parliament
Attempted self-immolations by students including the widely-covered case of Rajiv Goswami
Nationwide bandh and shutdown of universities for six months
Public burning of constitutional copies to protest against reservations

explanation: The anti-Mandal agitation produced shocking scenes of students attempting self-immolation to protest against OBC reservations. Rajiv Goswami, a Delhi University student, became the most widely covered case when he attempted to self-immolate in September 1990 and survived with severe burns. Several other students across India made similar attempts. These dramatic protests by upper-caste youth who feared reservation would reduce their job and educational opportunities generated massive media coverage and political controversy. The agitation revealed the deep anxieties of upper and middle-caste communities about their competitive position in an expanding reservation system.
elimination_hints: Burning of commission report copies was not the defining or most shocking protest method.
University shutdowns occurred but the defining emotional image was the self-immolation attempts.
Burning constitutional copies was not the form of protest; personal self-immolation attempts were the shocking image.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The Supreme Court upheld OBC reservations in the landmark Indra Sawhney case of 1992 but also established which important restriction on the scope of reservations?

options.text: Reservations cannot exceed 27 percent for any single community
Total reservations cannot ordinarily exceed 50 percent of available positions, and the creamy layer among OBCs must be excluded from reservation benefits
Reservations apply only to entry-level positions and not to promotions in government service
Reservations can only be applied in states where OBCs constitute more than 40 percent of the population

explanation: The Supreme Court's nine-judge bench in Indra Sawhney vs Union of India (1992) upheld the 27 percent OBC reservation but established two crucial limitations. First, it held that total reservations ordinarily cannot exceed 50 percent of available positions, making the combined SC/ST/OBC reservation of 49.5 percent just within bounds. Second, and most significantly for OBCs, it introduced the creamy layer exclusion: OBC individuals whose family income or social position had placed them in a relatively advanced category should be excluded from reservation benefits, so that reservations serve those most in need. This creamy layer concept has been central to reservation jurisprudence since 1992.
elimination_hints: The 27 percent limit was for OBCs overall, not per community; the 50 percent ceiling applies to total reservations.
Reservations in promotions was a separate issue addressed in subsequent cases; the 1992 case addressed the entry and 50 percent ceiling.
State-wise OBC population percentage was not the criterion set by the Indra Sawhney case.
subject: Political Science

question_text: V.P. Singh's decision to implement the Mandal Commission report in 1990 is analysed by political scientists as a political masterstroke that also had significant unintended consequences. What primarily drove V.P. Singh's timing?

options.text: V.P. Singh genuinely believed OBC reservation was a social justice priority that had been unfairly delayed since 1980

The announcement was partly a political tactic to consolidate OBC support and counter the BJP's growing Ram Mandir mobilisation, but it triggered anti-Mandal agitation that ultimately destabilised his government
V.P. Singh was pressured by the Left parties supporting his minority government to implement Mandal or lose their support
The announcement was required by a Supreme Court order that gave the government a deadline to implement Mandal recommendations

explanation: V.P. Singh's Mandal announcement in August 1990 is widely seen as having a dual motivation. Genuine social justice commitment may have played a role, but the timing was significantly shaped by political calculations. The BJP under Advani was gaining momentum with the Ram Mandir movement and Rath Yatra that threatened to consolidate Hindu upper-caste votes against the Janata Dal government. Mandal implementation was intended to create a competing political cleavage by mobilising OBC communities, who constituted a large electoral bloc, around their economic interests rather than Hindu identity. However, the resulting anti-Mandal agitation, the BJP's withdrawal of support following Advani's arrest, and the destabilisation of his government showed the strategy's backfire.
elimination_hints: Social justice motivation existed but cannot explain the 1990 timing when the report had been available since 1980.
Left parties supporting V.P. Singh did not specifically demand Mandal implementation as their condition.
No Supreme Court deadline required Mandal implementation in 1990; it was an executive choice.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The anti-Mandal agitation of 1990 was primarily led by upper-caste youth in North India. What does the geographic and social pattern of the agitation reveal about India's caste politics?

options.text: It reveals that upper-caste communities uniformly opposed any form of social justice for OBCs across all of India
It reveals the anxiety of upper and intermediate-caste communities in North India who had relied on government employment as a primary route of social mobility and now faced reduced access
It reveals that the anti-reservation movement was led by economically poor upper-caste communities who needed government jobs for survival
It reveals that South India was more progressive on caste issues than North India because no protests occurred there

explanation: The anti-Mandal agitation's concentration in North India and among upper and intermediate-caste students reflected the specific social position of these communities. In North India, central government employment had been a major avenue for middle-class aspiration and social mobility for forward-caste families. Unlike South India, which had implemented backward class reservations since the 1920s and where upper-caste communities had adjusted their aspirations accordingly, North Indian upper-caste families were confronting significant reservation for the first time. The students who protested were not the rural poor but predominantly urban educated youth from families whose social identity and economic aspiration were tied to competitive government service access.
elimination_hints: The agitation was geographically concentrated in North India and was not uniform across all of India.
The protestors were largely from economically comfortable families whose aspirations, not survival, were threatened.
South India had its own caste politics; the difference was that OBC reservations had existed there for decades.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The Mandal Commission controversy revealed a fundamental tension in Indian democracy between two competing principles. Which pair of principles best captures this tension?

options.text: Parliamentary sovereignty versus judicial review of reservation laws
The principle of equality of opportunity in a merit-based system versus the principle of social justice requiring special provisions to address structural inequality of opportunity
Federal distribution of powers versus central government's reservation authority
Religious minority rights versus majority Hindu community's claims in employment

explanation: The Mandal controversy crystallised a genuine tension at the heart of democratic equality. The anti-reservation argument invokes formal equality of opportunity: all citizens should compete equally on merit for government positions, and reservations by benefiting certain groups over others violate this principle. The pro-reservation argument invokes substantive equality: when centuries of discrimination have created structural disadvantages, formal equal opportunity produces unequal outcomes because the competitors do not start from the same position. Reservations are not violations of equality but corrections of inherited inequality that make genuine equal opportunity possible. This tension between formal and substantive equality, between individual merit and group-based historical justice, is the philosophical core of all reservation debates in India.
elimination_hints: Parliamentary versus judicial dynamics existed but were not the fundamental philosophical tension the controversy revealed.
Federal distribution was relevant for state-level reservations but not the core Mandal philosophical tension.
Religious minority rights were not the central issue in Mandal; the debate was about caste-based social backwardness.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Critics of the Mandal Commission report argued that it relied on the 1931 census for OBC identification and that economic criteria should replace caste as the basis for reservation. Critically evaluate this economic criterion argument.

options.text: The economic criterion argument is fully correct because poverty is the real problem and caste-based reservations only help relatively affluent OBCs

The economic criterion argument misunderstands the nature of OBC disadvantage which is social and cultural not merely economic, and would not address the specific dignity violations and discrimination that caste-based exclusion involves
Both caste and economic criteria are completely equivalent and either could equally address the reservation's purpose
The economic criterion argument is correct for urban areas but caste-based criteria are appropriate for rural areas

explanation: The economic criterion argument, while superficially appealing as universalist and non-divisive, misunderstands what reservations address. OBC and Dalit disadvantage is not merely economic poverty but involves specific social and cultural discrimination rooted in caste identity. An economically poor upper-caste person faces very different social treatment from an economically similar OBC or Dalit person: the latter faces caste-specific discrimination in hiring, social interaction, and dignity that the poor upper-caste person does not. Economic reservations would benefit poor upper-caste communities without addressing the specific caste-based discrimination that the reservation policy targets. Additionally, if economic criteria were used, upper-caste communities with similar income levels to OBCs would compete for the same reserved positions, defeating the purpose of addressing caste discrimination specifically.
elimination_hints: Poverty alone does not capture the additional dimension of caste-specific social discrimination and dignity violations.
Caste and economic criteria are not equivalent because caste involves specific forms of discrimination beyond economic disadvantage.
The rural-urban distinction does not resolve the core issue about whether economic or caste criteria better target caste discrimination.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The Mandal controversy's long-term political consequence for India's party system was described as producing a 'second democratic upsurge'. What does this concept mean in the Indian context?

options.text: A second wave of pro-democracy protests against authoritarian governance similar to the 1977 anti-Emergency wave
The political mobilisation of OBC communities that transformed the social base of Indian democracy from upper-caste dominated to more genuinely representative of India's social majority
A second round of constitutional amendments strengthening democratic institutions after the Emergency
The rise of a second major national party system to replace Congress's single-party dominance

explanation: The second democratic upsurge, a concept used by scholars like Yogendra Yadav, refers to the political awakening and mobilisation of OBC communities that Mandal catalysed. The first democratic upsurge was the initial mass participation in elections by previously marginalised communities during the 1950s-1960s. The second upsurge, accelerated by Mandal, involved OBC communities developing stronger political consciousness, forming or supporting parties that specifically represented their interests, and demanding their share of political power. Parties like the Samajwadi Party, Rashtriya Janata Dal, and regional OBC-dominated parties emerged or strengthened. This transformation democratised India's political class by reducing upper-caste overrepresentation in elected offices, making the political system more genuinely representative of India's social composition.
elimination_hints: Second democratic upsurge is not about anti-authoritarian protest; it is about social group political mobilisation.
Constitutional amendments are not the referent of the concept; it refers to social group political participation.
Party system change was a consequence of the upsurge but the concept refers to social group political mobilisation.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The Mandal issue split Indian society in 1990 along caste lines but subsequently saw the consolidation of OBC political power. Critically assess whether the Mandal implementation ultimately strengthened or weakened Indian democracy.

options.text: Mandal weakened Indian democracy by permanently dividing society along caste lines and preventing national unity

Mandal strengthened Indian democracy by making the political system more socially representative, expanding the circle of political participation, and giving previously marginalised communities a stake in democratic institutions
Mandal had no effect on democracy because reservations are an administrative matter unrelated to democratic participation
Mandal weakened democracy by replacing merit-based governance with caste-based patronage systems

explanation: The most defensible assessment is that Mandal implementation strengthened Indian democracy's representative character despite the social conflict it generated. A democracy where political and administrative power is systematically concentrated in communities comprising roughly 15-20 percent of the population while the majority is underrepresented is structurally limited in its democratic quality. Mandal's implementation contributed to the entry of OBC communities into political and administrative leadership. The rise of OBC chief ministers, MPs, and IAS officers from these communities expanded who participates in and exercises democratic power. The social conflict generated by Mandal, though painful, was the conflict of social groups making legitimate democratic claims rather than the conflict of anti-democratic forces resisting democratic governance.
elimination_hints: While caste polarisation is a real concern, the alternative was continued upper-caste dominance of democratic institutions.
Reservations are directly related to democratic representation by determining who accesses power and administrative positions.
Merit-based governance is itself undermined when only dominant caste groups have access to education and employment networks.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Match the key events and terms in Column A with their correct descriptions in Column B.
explanation: The Mandal Commission report recommended 27 percent reservation for OBCs and was submitted in 1980. V.P. Singh's August 1990 announcement to implement the report triggered the anti-Mandal agitation. The Indra Sawhney Supreme Court judgment of 1992 upheld OBC reservations while establishing the 50 percent ceiling and creamy layer exclusion. The creamy layer concept specifically excludes OBC individuals and families who have reached a relatively advanced economic or social position from accessing reservation benefits.
elimination_hints: Mandal Commission report was the foundational document with the 27 percent recommendation.
V.P. Singh's announcement specifically triggered the agitation; the report itself was submitted 10 years earlier.
Creamy layer is specifically about excluding relatively privileged OBCs, not about the total ceiling.
subject: Political Science

column_b.text: Upper-income OBCs excluded from reservation benefits to direct support to most needy
Supreme Court upheld OBC reservations but set 50 percent ceiling and creamy layer rule
Recommendation of 27 percent central government jobs for OBCs; submitted 1980
Implementation of Mandal recommendations triggering anti-reservation agitation

question_text: Match the political parties and leaders in Column A with their position on the Mandal Commission in Column B.
explanation: V.P. Singh and Janata Dal used Mandal implementation as a social justice move that also served to counter the BJP's Ram Mandir mobilisation by creating a competing political cleavage. BJP withdrew support from V.P. Singh partly over Advani's arrest during the Rath Yatra and shifted its mobilisation focus to the Ram Mandir issue rather than directly opposing reservations. Congress had historically been ambivalent about Mandal implementation, having not implemented it during Rajiv Gandhi's tenure despite having the report since 1980. Left parties generally supported anti-caste measures but with reservations (no pun intended) about whether caste-based rather than class-based approaches were more effective.
elimination_hints: V.P. Singh was the implementer whose political calculations included countering the BJP.
BJP's response was specifically to withdraw support and redirect to Ram Mandir rather than direct opposition to reservations.
Left parties' support was qualified by their class-based analysis of caste problems.
subject: Political Science

column_b.text: Broadly supportive of OBC reservations but had been reluctant to implement Mandal themselves when in power
Withdrew support from V.P. Singh government; focused on Ram Mandir as alternative Hindu unity agenda
Implemented Mandal to consolidate OBC support and counter BJP's Ram Mandir mobilisation
Supported Mandal as part of broader anti-caste class-based politics but noted limitations of caste-based approach

question_text: Match the consequences of the Mandal controversy in Column A with their long-term political effects in Column B.
explanation: V.P. Singh's government fall demonstrated the short-term political cost of social justice implementation while the OBC political mobilisation it triggered proved electorally transformative. The rise of Samajwadi Party under Mulayam Singh Yadav and RJD under Lalu Prasad Yadav represents the second democratic upsurge of OBC political representation. Anti-Mandal upper-caste mobilisation channelled into BJP support, contributing to its emergence as the primary vehicle for forward-caste communities in North India. The creamy layer exclusion has created an increasingly contested distinction between OBCs who still qualify for reservation and those excluded as relatively privileged, generating new internal OBC political debates.
elimination_hints: V.P. Singh's government fall showed both short-term cost and long-term electoral transformation.
SP and RJD specifically represent the OBC second democratic upsurge in North Indian state politics.
Anti-Mandal agitation contributed specifically to BJP's social base consolidation.
subject: Political Science

column_a.text: Fall of V.P. Singh government in November 1990
Rise of OBC-based regional parties like SP and RJD
Creamy layer exclusion by Supreme Court

column_b.text: Created internal OBC differentiation between those who still qualify and a growing excluded segment
Contributed to BJP's emergence as the vehicle for upper and intermediate-caste communities
Demonstrated that social justice measures could be politically destabilising in short term but electorally transformative
Second democratic upsurge making North Indian state politics more OBC-representative

question_text: Assertion (A): The Mandal Commission's recommendation of 27 percent reservation for OBCs was based on the finding that OBCs constitute approximately 52 percent of India's population. Reasoning (R): The commission argued that OBCs' share of government employment should reflect their population share, but applied a discount to keep total reservations within constitutional bounds.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are accurate and causally connected. The Mandal Commission estimated OBC population at approximately 52 percent based on the 1931 census data (the last census to record caste comprehensively). Logically, proportional representation would have given OBCs a much larger reservation, but when added to the existing 22.5 percent SC/ST reservation, total reservations would have exceeded 50 percent which the commission anticipated would be constitutionally challenged. The 27 percent figure was therefore a discounted recommendation that would bring total reservations to 49.5 percent. The Reasoning correctly identifies the mathematical logic: population proportion minus the discount needed to stay within anticipated constitutional limits.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Assertion (A): The simultaneous announcement of Mandal and the BJP's Ram Mandir Rath Yatra in 1990 represented a clash between two competing visions of Indian identity: caste-based social justice versus Hindu religious unity. Reasoning (R): Mandal mobilised communities along economic-social caste lines while the Rath Yatra sought to unify all Hindus across caste lines around a shared religious identity, making them structurally opposed political strategies.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are accurate and the Reasoning provides an insightful explanation of the Assertion. The 1990 political moment captured a fundamental tension in Indian identity politics. Mandal's implementation attempted to mobilise OBC communities around their specific socioeconomic caste interests, emphasising the diversity and hierarchy within Hinduism. The Rath Yatra aimed to transcend internal Hindu caste divisions by creating a pan-Hindu religious solidarity around the Ram Janmabhoomi movement. These were structurally opposed because Mandal highlighted the distinctions within the Hindu community while the Rath Yatra tried to erase those distinctions under a unified Hindu identity. Political scientists have described this as the Mandal-Kamandal contradiction at the heart of Indian politics in the early 1990s.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Assertion (A): South India implemented backward class reservations decades before North India, which explains why the anti-Mandal agitation was primarily a North Indian phenomenon. Reasoning (R): South Indian upper-caste communities had already adjusted to sharing government employment with OBC communities since the 1920s-50s, so the Mandal announcement represented less of a shock to their expectations.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are historically accurate and the Reasoning correctly explains the regional difference. South Indian states including Madras Province had implemented backward class reservations beginning in the 1920s in response to non-Brahmin movements. By 1990, South Indian upper-caste communities had lived alongside OBC reservation systems for generations and had adjusted their occupational and educational aspirations accordingly, often by moving into private sector employment and self-employment. The Mandal announcement applied to central government jobs, not state governments, and represented a new imposition on North Indian upper-caste communities who had not previously faced such competition in the central government sphere that was their primary aspiration. This historical adjustment explains the differential regional intensity of the agitation.
subject: Political Science

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the Mandal controversy in correct chronological order:
1. Indra Sawhney Supreme Court judgment upholding OBC reservations with creamy layer condition
2. V.P. Singh government announces implementation of Mandal Commission recommendations
3. Mandal Commission submits its report to the government
4. Anti-Mandal agitation with student self-immolations across North India
5. V.P. Singh government falls; Chandra Shekhar becomes Prime Minister
explanation: The correct sequence is: Mandal Commission submits report in 1980 (during Indira Gandhi's government which did not implement it); V.P. Singh announces implementation in August 1990; anti-Mandal agitation erupts including self-immolation attempts in September-October 1990; V.P. Singh government falls in November 1990 after BJP withdraws support over Advani's arrest; Supreme Court's Indra Sawhney judgment in 1992 upholds OBC reservations while setting the creamy layer exclusion. This sequence shows the decade-long delay between the report and implementation, and the rapid political consequences of implementation.
elimination_hints: Mandal Commission report submission in 1980 was the first event, a decade before implementation.
Supreme Court judgment in 1992 came after the agitation and government fall, not before.
V.P. Singh's announcement preceded the agitation which was a response to the announcement.
subject: Political Science

question_text:
Arrange the following political effects of the Mandal controversy from the most immediate short-term to the most significant long-term consequence:
1. OBC communities develop distinct political consciousness and support OBC-based parties
2. V.P. Singh government falls within months of announcing Mandal implementation
3. Second democratic upsurge transforms North Indian state politics with OBC chief ministers
4. Upper-caste communities consolidate behind BJP as their political vehicle
5. Anti-Mandal student agitation destabilises North Indian cities
explanation: The sequence from most immediate to most long-term runs: anti-Mandal agitation was the most immediate response occurring within weeks of the announcement; V.P. Singh government's fall in November 1990 was the next most immediate political consequence; upper-caste consolidation behind BJP developed over the following years; OBC communities developing distinct political consciousness and party support grew over several years; the second democratic upsurge transforming North Indian politics was the most significant long-term consequence visible across decades. This sequence shows how an immediate political crisis generated consequences that progressively grew in scale and transformed Indian democracy.
elimination_hints: Anti-Mandal student agitation was the most immediate response, occurring within weeks.
The second democratic upsurge is the long-term structural consequence spanning decades.
V.P. Singh's government fall was rapid, within three months of the announcement.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Identify which of the following statements about the Mandal Commission and OBC reservations is TRUE: (I) The Mandal Commission was constituted during the Janata Party government in 1978. (II) The Indra Sawhney judgment established that the total reservation cannot exceed 50 percent except in extraordinary circumstances. (III) The Mandal Commission recommended reservations for OBCs in private sector employment as its primary demand. (IV) V.P. Singh was the Prime Minister who implemented the Mandal Commission report in 1990.

options.text: Statements I, II and IV
Statements I and II
Statements II and IV
All four statements

explanation: Statements I, II, and IV are all true. The Mandal Commission was constituted in December 1978 during the Janata Party government under Morarji Desai. The Indra Sawhney judgment did establish the 50 percent ceiling as the ordinary limit for reservations with exceptional circumstances as the only basis for exceeding it. V.P. Singh was indeed the Prime Minister who announced Mandal implementation in August 1990. Statement III is false: the Mandal Commission's primary recommendation was for reservations in central government services and public sector undertakings, not private sector employment. Extension to the private sector has been a subsequent demand made by OBC groups and discussed in later political discourse.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Study the diagram showing the Mandal-Kamandal political matrix with the four quadrants representing different combinations of caste identity and Hindu religious identity in Indian politics. Based on this diagram, which conclusion best explains why the 1990s produced such dramatic political realignment in India?

options.text: The realignment occurred because all political parties changed their ideologies simultaneously in 1990

The Mandal-Kamandal matrix created a structural realignment where communities had to choose between caste-economic identity and Hindu-religious identity, sorting voters and parties into two competing blocs that replaced the older Congress synthesis
The realignment was caused entirely by the economic liberalisation of 1991 which changed voters' priorities
The realignment was temporary and Indian politics returned to its pre-1990 configuration within a decade

explanation: The diagram captures the Mandal-Kamandal dynamic that structurally realigned Indian politics. Before 1990, Congress's success relied on an improbable synthesis combining upper-caste support with SC/ST and Muslim minority support, keeping OBCs within a broad Congress tent. Mandal's implementation activated OBC communities' specific economic-caste identity, making them demand their own political representation rather than accepting Congress's umbrella. Simultaneously, the BJP's Ram Mandir mobilisation offered upper-caste and intermediate-caste communities a Hindu identity alternative to caste-specific competition. This created two competing mobilisation strategies: caste-economic identity for OBCs and Hindu religious identity for forward castes. The Congress synthesis that had held both together collapsed under this structural pressure, producing the fragmented multi-party system of the 1990s.
elimination_hints: Different parties took different positions on Mandal and Kamandal; simultaneous ideology change is not accurate.
Economic liberalisation was a concurrent change but the political realignment was specifically structured by the Mandal-Kamandal dynamic.
The political transformation proved durable; Indian politics did not return to pre-1990 Congress dominance.
subject: Political Science
